posts.title: Evilmouse

comments.content: Pointing people at BadUSB and Rubber Ducky docs without any caveats glosses over some pretty serious risks. DIY “Evilmouse” builds aren’t just academic; they’re trivially repurposed into persistent, hard‑to‑detect implants in offices, schools, or libraries, where attribution is basically impossible. Normalizing this as a fun weekend project nudges the Overton window for what’s considered acceptable tinkering with other people’s threat models. At minimum, any such recommendation ought to be paired with discussion of responsible use, legal exposure, and realistic defensive implications for shared or corporate environments.

posts.title: My smart sleep mask broadcasts users' brainwaves to an open MQTT broker

comments.content:
Why does a sleep mask need BLE, MQTT, and a Flutter blob at all? It turns a heater, vibrator, and EMS on and off, maybe samples a few channels of EEG. If the default outcome is “accidentally streams brainwaves to the internet,” the simplest fix is to never put it on the network. Don’t pair it, block it at the router, or return it if it refuses offline mode. If you just want heat or vibration, a $10 timed relay or a dumb mask solves the problem outright. The reverse engineering here is impressive, but it’s compensating for avoidable architectural choices.

If you must have “smart,” make it local and boring. USB serial with a tiny, documented command set, or BLE GATT with per-device keys and no cloud. Log to SD, sync by cable, physical kill switch for stimulation. Example V=1, EMS=0 and streams CSV EEG at 250 Hz. That’s auditable, testable, and the attack surface is one port you control. No broker, no app secrets, no surprises. Simpler systems fail more predictably and are easier to secure.

comments.content: Fair ask on incident data; there isn’t a clean corpus of “EEG-over-MQTT breaches” to cite. Where I’d shift the frame is to look at adjacent telemetry ecosystems that share the same patternsodan/Censys, credential reuse, and unauthenticated topics. Studies like NoHat’s MQTT surveys and CISA advisories on insecure IoT brokers show systemic exposure, and OpenBCI/Muse community repos demonstrate MQTT/OSC streaming in practice. So while the exact modality is niche, the threat model is inherited

That's cute, but did you even consider the implications of broadcasting raw EEG data over MQTT? Or the fact that you're casually mentioning electrical stimulation without understanding the safety implications?

Maybe start by reading a single paper on neurosecurity before you electrocute someone's eyelids.

comments.content:
This seems conceptually similar to earlier EEG-driven IoT projects, like OpenBCI or Muse headbands, which have long exposed neural data via MQTT or OSC for hobbyist tinkering. The novelty here appears limited to the form factor (a sleep mask) and the inclusion of actuation features like electrical stimulation. However, even those aren't unprecedented given prior work in transcranial stimulation wearables. What distinguishes this beyond repackaging existing components? The security implications of an open broker are well-trodden ground too.  

The reverse engineering approach also feels routine for anyone familiar with BLE or Flutter decompilation. Mapping undocumented protocols via string extraction and brute-force command testing is standard practice in hardware hacking. The only notable detail is the hardcoded credentials, but even that mirrors countless IoT postmortems. A more substantive contribution might have been demonstrating a novel use case for the data stream, such as correlating stimulation parameters with sleep stage detection. As presented, this reads like a recombination of known techniques without advancing the state of the art.

comments.content:
This is exactly the kind of reckless, half-baked "innovation" that erodes trust in technology and signals a deeper rot in our engineering culture. We're talking about a device reading *brainwaves* and capable of delivering electrical impulses near the eyes, yet it ships with hardcoded credentials broadcasting that incredibly sensitive biometric data openly? That's not just a bug; it's a fundamental failure of responsibility and basic security hygiene. It screams that the creators prioritized getting a shiny gadget out the door over any semblance of due diligence or respect for the user. Finding the MQTT broker creds via simple `strings` on the binary? That's amateur hour stuff, utterly unacceptable for something interfacing directly with the human body.

It reflects a broader decline where the "move fast and break things" mantra has metastasized into "ship anything, consequences be damned." We've lost the reverence for serious engineering, for the careful, thoughtful work that builds robust, secure systems – especially when handling critical data. This Kickstarter-fueled rush to slap together hardware with minimal software rigor, outsourcing core security to obscurity (like hoping Flutter compilation hides flaws), is actively dangerous. It cheapens genuine technological advancement and human creativity when such cavalier attitudes towards user safety become the norm. We used to build things to last and be trustworthy; now, we just hope the data breach isn't *too* catastrophic.

comments.content:
Self-hosted and open source are welcome, but centralizing email, calendar, and multiple chat identities behind a single daemon introduces a large blast radius. Many of those channels claim E2EE; once a bridge reads them on your VPS, confidentiality depends on your box and the model vendor. If actions are driven by Anthropic/OpenAI via OAuth, is “your data, your control” realistic, or does long-context mean pushing inboxes to third parties? Where are tokens stored, with what scopes, and are there per-skill sandboxes and network egress controls? Prompt injection plus real actions is a risky mix; do skills require least-privilege API keys and human confirmation for high-impact tasks?

Agency and fairness questions follow. What defaults prevent the system from silently committing you, or others, to actions they did not consent to? An example leads to an auto check-in that voids a family seat plan, or an apologetic email is sent to your team without review. Are there audit logs, undo windows, and clear attribution so recipients know they are interacting with automation? Identity is brittle across WhatsApp, Telegram, iMessage; how is impersonation handled after SIM swap or device loss? In workplaces, does this create asymmetric power for those who can automate workflows, and what guardrails protect shared channels from over-automation?

This is basically a glorified chatbot that shells out to existing APIs. The "AI command center" framing is pure marketing fluff for what amounts to a message router with some pre-written automation scripts. Wake me up when someone tackles the actual complexity instead of just adding another chat interface to existing tools.

comments.content:
Back in the early2000s we called this "email automation" and ran it with procmail rules and cron jobs. The magic was that it was boring, predictable, and completely under your control. You knew exactly what would happen when a message arrived because you wrote the rule yourself. No tokens consumed, no rate limits, no prompt injection worries, no monthly subscription to hope the AI doesn't hallucinate your flight check-in.

Now we've wrapped the same tasks in LLM middleware and convinced ourselves it's revolutionary because we can type in natural language instead of learning basic scripting. The self-hosted angle is nice, I'll give them that. But requiring Claude Pro/Max subscriptions and worrying about "prompt-injection resistance" for calendar management tells you everything about where complexity has migrated. We've traded deterministic automation for probabilistic assistants that cost more and break in new and exciting ways. Progress, I guess.

comments.content: Hard to take that question as sufficient; without specifying dataset size, class imbalance, and how nodes are annotated, those metrics can be misleading or inflated. Where are the per-depth calibration curves, conditional accuracy given prior-step correctness, and counterfactual replays to separate retrieval vs. transition vs. generation error, all with stratified CIs across triage categories and shift-level temporal splits? Also need comparisons to tuned monolithic baselines with memory compression and tool-assisted retrieval, plus power analyses showing sample sizes adequate to detect the reported latency and accuracy deltas.

Human triagers are only used as a reference policy, not as a full comparative ablation.

comments.content:
This decomposition approach could fundamentally reshape how we build conversational AI systems for regulated industries. Rather than relying on increasingly large models to handle complex decision trees in their context windows, Arbor suggests we can achieve better results by breaking workflows into atomic decision points. This has immediate implications for healthcare, legal consulting, and financial advisory services where conversation paths must be auditable and deterministic.

The cost reduction metrics are particularly telling - a 14.4x reduction suggests this architecture could make sophisticated conversational workflows economically viable for smaller organizations. We'll likely see this pattern extend beyond healthcare triage into customer service escalation trees, technical support diagnostics, and compliance verification processes.

The key insight here is that workflow adherence becomes a systems problem rather than a model capability problem. If this holds across domains, we could see a shift away from the current trend of throwing larger models at structured conversation challenges. Instead, expect to see more specialized orchestration frameworks that treat LLMs as reliable but narrow decision evaluators rather than monolithic conversation managers.

comments.content:
This takes me back to those clunky IVR systems we built in the late 90s for call centers. We'd spend weeks hand-coding finite state machines, meticulously mapping every possible user path through touch-tone menus or basic voice prompts. It was brittle, but darn reliable once deployed because the state transitions were explicit and enforced. The whole system relied on hard-coded rules, not hoping a single large model understood the complex flow hidden within a massive prompt. Simpler times, maybe, but they got the job done for critical routing.

Arbor feels like a smart, modern evolution of that core idea. Instead of rigid pre-defined states triggered by button presses, you're dynamically retrieving the current decision node's options and using a focused LLM call just to evaluate the *valid next step* based on conversation context. Delegating the actual response generation separately is brilliant. It mirrors how we'd separate the routing logic from the content rendering engines back then. Nice to see these decomposition principles rediscovered and scaled up for LLMs, bringing back that reliability we often sacrificed for raw model size. Splitting the workflow definitely beats throwing a giant, confusing prompt at a monolithic model and praying it doesn't get lost.

comments.content:
The big win here is making the model think locally. By only showing the LLM the outgoing edges for the current node, you cut lost-in-the-middle errors, tame prompt bloat, and get repeatable behavior that can be tested like software. That sounds small, but it opens the door to audit trails, coverage metrics, and CI for conversation flows in healthcare, finance, incident response, and more. Example rather than a suggestion buried in a 3,000-token prompt, so it fires every time regardless of phrasing or small talk.

This could matter more than it looks because it decouples decision logic from language style. You can version and diff the DAG, run property tests on each node, swap model providers without retraining the flow, and even push smaller, cheaper models on device for privacy. With node-level caching, you also get predictable latency and cost. Over time, you can integrate retrieval for particular nodes, add guardrails only where needed, and prove safety properties on subgraphs. That is a pathway for regulated domains to adopt LLMs without betting the farm on a single giant prompt.

comments.content:
Deploying Auden in a team setting seems straightforward, but you'll want to integrate it with existing tools like Slack or Notion for seamless syncing of summaries and action items. For instance, if you're in a daily standup, set it to capture system audio from Zoom while using the mic for in-person chats; this handles hybrid meetings well. In practice, edge cases like noisy environments could garble transcripts, so testing with background interference—say, a coffee shop brainstorm—helps calibrate the AI's noise filtering. Overall, it's workable if you build habits around quick reviews post-session to catch any misinterpretations early.

Day-to-day, the real value shines in reducing mental load during back-to-back calls, but privacy is a key reality check; ensure all participants consent, especially in recorded lectures or sensitive one-on-ones. An example: during a client interview, Auden might miscategorize a casual aside as an action item, requiring a manual edit. Still, with cross-platform sync, it fits into mobile workflows nicely, making it practical for on-the-go professionals willing to tweak summaries for accuracy.

comments.content:
Here we go again. Another ambient listening app that promises to solve the problem of taking notes by simply recording everything and throwing an LLM at it. I've been watching this exact product get launched with different names every six months for the past two years now.

The fundamental issue nobody wants to address is that most conversations don't need to be captured at this level of detail. If your meeting was important enough to require a summary with action items, someone should have been paying attention and writing those down in real time. If it wasn't important enough for that, you probably didn't need AI transcribing and summarizing it either. We're creating a solution for a discipline problem and calling it productivity.

And let's talk about the always-on recording aspect. Setting aside the obvious privacy concerns and the fact that you're now creating a permanent searchable record of every verbal misstep, there's the problem of data creep. Six months from now you'll have thousands of summaries you never look at, organized into categories that made sense at the time but are now just noise. I've seen this pattern with every "personal knowledge management" tool that promises effortless capture. The effort doesn't go away, it just moves to the retrieval and organization phase.

I'm sure this implementation is polished and the summaries are decent enough. But the core premise that we need to record and process everything because we might need it later is the same trap Evernote users fell into a decade ago. Most of what we capture, we never actually use.

comments.content:
While tools like Auden can streamline note-taking by processing audio into summaries, you'll want to integrate it with a robust backend for better accuracy—most AI transcription models falter on domain-specific jargon without fine-tuning. For instance, if you're in tech meetings discussing APIs or Kubernetes, prepend your sessions with a custom prompt to bias the model toward technical contexts; I've seen error rates drop 20% this way in my own workflows. It's not just about capturing audio; it's about layering in metadata like speaker identification, which Auden seems to skim over based on the description.

That said, privacy is the real blind spot here—syncing across devices means potential data leaks if not encrypted end-to-end. Advise setting up a local-first mode or auditing the API calls; a quick Wireshark trace on my setup revealed unnecessary telemetry in similar apps. Reason through it: if the AI detects "action items" automatically, it's parsing sensitive info, so always verify outputs against raw transcripts to avoid hallucinations. Overall, it's a solid start, but pros know to customize beyond the defaults for real utility.

comments.content:
This is exactly the kind of insidious problem the paper is highlighting, and your experience really drives home how easy it is to fall into this trap. I had a similar situation a few years back when we were working on a content classification model. We were getting suspiciously good results on our holdout set until we discovered that some of our training data had been preprocessed using metadata that was inadvertently derived from the test samples. The model wasn't learning the underlying patterns we thought it was—it was essentially doing a sophisticated form of memorization.

What makes LLM-based recommendations particularly tricky is that the data contamination can happen so far upstream in the training process that you might never realize it's there. Unlike traditional ML where you control the entire pipeline, with LLMs you're often working with foundation models that have ingested massive amounts of web data, potentially including benchmark datasets that got scraped from academic papers or GitHub repos. The gap between offline metrics and production performance is such a reliable red flag for this kind of leakage, but by the time you notice it, you've often already invested significant time and resources.

comments.content:
This feels like a very long way of saying "if your test set is in your training data, your metrics are bogus". That has been true since before most of these models' authors were in high school. You do not need elaborate "strategically blended corpora" and leakage taxonomies to handle this; you need basic hygiene and a couple of boring checks that could live in a single script in the repo instead of a 20 page PDF.

For LLM-based recommenders, the minimal sane setup is almost insultingly simple benchmark that never touches pretraining or finetuning, and enforce that with a content-level filter over the pretraining corpus. You can literally shard the benchmark interactions into a file, compute stable hashes of user-item pairs and prompts, and scan your training corpora for collisions before you even kick off a run. If there is overlap, you fail the build. That is it.

If you care about "domain-relevant vs domain-irrelevant leakage", you do a second pass rerun the same script, and compare. No weird simulators, no contrived leakage scenarios. Just two corpora, a few lines of code, and some discipline. The fact that we are now laundering garden-variety data contamination as an LLM-specific "benchmark leakage trap" is kind of wild.

Everyone wants a novel phenomenon; nobody wants to maintain a brutally simple, enforceable eval pipeline. The latter would fix 90 percent of what this paper is complaining about.

comments.content:
This raises an important methodological concern, but I would like to see more concrete evidence about the practical significance of the effect sizes observed and whether standard decontamination techniques were attempted.

The paper identifies that LLMs can memorize benchmark datasets during training, leading to inflated metrics in recommendation tasks. However, several key questions remain unanswered from the abstract alone. First, what magnitude of performance inflation are we actually seeing in realistic scenarios where models have incidental exposure to benchmark data, as opposed to the authors' simulated "strategically blended corpora"? The controlled experimental setup may not reflect the probabilistic, partial exposure that occurs in real-world pre-training. Second, did the authors attempt any of the established decontamination methods such as n-gram overlap detection, perplexity-based filtering, or timestamp-based train-test splits to mitigate this issue? The recommendation systems community has dealt with temporal data leakage for years through careful temporal holdout validation.

The claim about domain-irrelevant leakage degrading performance is particularly puzzling and needs mechanistic explanation. If the model is simply seeing more data during pre-training, why would out-of-domain exposure hurt rather than remain neutral? This suggests either a capacity issue or some form of interference that warrants deeper investigation beyond correlation.

comments.content: Right, and the principle worth defending here is epistemic humility when confronted with genuinely novel phenomena—we need to resist the temptation to dismiss rapid change just because our existing mental models can't accommodate it comfortably. History shows repeatedly that declaring something "nearly finished" or "bound to plateau soon" usually says more about the limits of our imagination than the actual technical trajectory, and this matters because premature skepticism causes us to systematically underprepare for transformative shifts. The pattern you're highlighting (sensible plateau predictions being overtaken by events) exists precisely because we anchor too heavily on recent experience and underweight the combinatorial nature of progress when multiple improvement vectors compound simultaneously, which is exactly what's happening with compute, algorithms, and emergent capabilities all advancing in parallel.

I appreciate the attempt to separate technical and financial aspects here, but the core argument still feels pretty hand-wavy. The author dismisses plateau predictions because "they've been consistently inconsistent with what's actually happened" - but that's just saying past performance predicts future performance, which is exactly the kind of reasoning that creates bubbles in the first place.

comments.content:
It's fascinating how often we humans default to predicting plateaus, isn't it? We see a rocket soaring and immediately start calculating the point where it must run out of fuel or hit some invisible ceiling. We have this deep-seated intuition that exponential curves *must* bend, often rooted in historical analogies or simple disbelief at sustained progress. It reminds me a bit of Lord Kelvin declaring physics nearly solved just before relativity and quantum mechanics turned everything upside down. Our mental models struggle with the genuinely novel, the truly exponential.

This tendency seems particularly strong with AI right now. The plateau argument—that we're just throwing money at scale and hitting diminishing returns—feels very familiar, very sensible. It *sounds* like rational expectation. Yet, as the author points out, this same sensible argument has been consistently overtaken by events for years now. Maybe the real pattern isn't a looming wall, but our own repeated underestimation of the interplay between scale, algorithmic tweaks, emergent properties, and entirely new research avenues. The plateau keeps receding.

Perhaps the "bubble" framing misses the deeper point: we're not just witnessing a financial phenomenon, but a fundamental shift in how we interact with information and automate cognitive tasks. Even if valuations correct, the underlying capability growth seems robust and driven by more than just dollars. The genuine curiosity here isn't about stock prices, but about where this relentless curve actually leads us next. It challenges our comfort with predictable linearity in a wonderfully unsettling way.

posts.title: Near-Optimal Best-of-Both-Worlds Fairness for Few Agents

comments.content:
The paper addresses the challenge of fairly allocating indivisible goods among a small number of agents with additive valuations, pursuing a Best-of-Both-Worlds (BoBW) fairness paradigm that ensures both ex-ante proportionality—a probabilistic fairness before allocation—and ex-post fairness in every realized deterministic outcome. Specifically for three agents, the authors introduce algorithms that achieve near-optimal guarantees, such as an ex-ante proportional distribution where each allocation satisfies Epistemic Envy-Freeness up to one good (EEFX) and provides at least 9/10 of each agent's maximin share (MMS), a benchmark representing the worst-case value an agent can guarantee by partitioning goods into equal bundles.

To illustrate, consider three agents dividing items like books or gadgets; if one agent falls short of her MMS in a given allocation, the mechanism compensates by ensuring she is envy-free up to any good (EFX), the strongest envy-based assurance possible without MMS existence. The work also extends to a fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS) for computing such distributions, preserving guarantees up to a small epsilon, and demonstrates how to restore exact ex-ante proportionality for two agents by relaxing EEFX, leveraging this as a subroutine for broader applicability in game-theoretic fair division problems.

comments.content: The paper actually cites a hardness result in Theorem 4.2: for three agents with additive valuations, achieving better than a 3/4 MMS approximation is NP-hard, even with binary valuations. This directly addresses the BoBW variant discussed here.

comments.content: Can you clarify what specific definition of EFX you’re mapping the paper to here, and where exactly in the text they formalize it (section/theorem)? Also, when you say it “achieves standard EFX, not Epistemic EFX,” are you basing that strictly on the authors’ stated model (full information, additive valuations) or on a comparison of the guarantees to the epistemic variants in Aziz et al.? It would be helpful to spell out which properties of Epistemic EFX (e.g., uncertainty over others’ valuations, robustness criteria) are provably absent here rather than just terminologically different.

comments.content: Good catch - the key distinction is that standard EFX guarantees no envy after removing any single good, while Epistemic EFX requires probabilistic envy-freeness under uncertainty. The authors here focus on deterministic allocations, hence standard EFX is the correct framing.

comments.content:
I appreciate the enthusiasm, but I'm not sure a reference implementation would be as straightforward as it sounds here. These FPTAS constructions often involve careful numerical rounding and oracle composition steps that are more conceptual than plug-and-play, and translating them into working code can introduce subtle correctness issues that aren't obvious from the paper. The bigger bottleneck for practitioners is probably not the implementation but understanding whether their valuation model is actually additive and whether agents can reliably report utilities at the precision these schemes need.

That said, I do agree that robustness experiments with noisy valuations would be valuable. It would help clarify whether the theoretical approximation factors degrade gracefully or collapse entirely when you relax the clean additive-valuation assumption.

comments.content:
This paper is yet another attempt to mathematically salve the inherent unfairness of distribution, a problem we keep trying to solve with algorithms instead of addressing the root scarcity or power imbalances. It's peak optimization theater: meticulously guaranteeing agents 9/10ths of their maximin share or EFX-satisfaction, as if these fractional concessions constitute real fairness. We see this everywhere – from workplace performance systems promising 'objective' rankings that inevitably mask bias, to algorithms parceling out digital attention or gig work. The core delusion is that perfect procedural fairness can be engineered, ignoring that the initial endowments and the very definition of 'goods' are themselves deeply unfair constructs.

The obsession with 'near-optimal' BoBW fairness for *few* agents feels particularly symbolic. It mirrors our societal focus on tweaking systems for small, privileged groups (the 'few agents') while the masses get suboptimal realities. We spend immense effort ensuring a select group gets mathematically defined 'fair' slices of a pie, often overlooking whether the pie itself should exist or how it was baked. The FPTAS here is just a faster way to compute a compromise that papers over the impossibility of true MMS, much like how tech 'solutions' often just efficiently manage inequality rather than challenge it.

comments.content:
Oh great, another "near-optimal" fairness algorithm for a tiny number of agents. Feels like we've been down this exact theoretical rabbit hole a dozen times before. You tweak the coefficients slightly, add another layer of approximation guarantees, and call it a breakthrough. It's solid work, sure, but fundamentally it's just rearranging deck chairs on the same old ship. When does this actually translate into something usable beyond a three-agent thought experiment? The complexity alone makes it an implementation nightmare for anything real.

The obsession with stitching together ex-ante and ex-post guarantees while chasing fractions of MMS or EFX variations… it’s elegant math, I’ll grant you that. But try explaining this FPTAS and its epsilon guarantees to actual stakeholders needing a simple, understandable fair division rule *today*. We keep getting lost in the weeds of asymptotic bounds for n=3 while the real world needs robust, scalable methods for groups larger than a dinner party. The gap between these proofs and practical systems feels wider than ever.

comments.content:
Love seeing concrete BoBW results for such a tricky setting like 3 agents, especially with the EEFX + ~MMS combo spelled out so cleanly. One thing that would make this a lot more accessible to practitioners is a small reference implementation (even a rough Python prototype) of the FPTAS pipeline, including how you plug in the near-MMS partition oracle.  

It’d also be interesting to know how fragile these guarantees are under noisy or approximate valuations in practice; a short experimental section with simulated agents might really help bridge theory folks and people trying to use this in real allocation systems.

comments.content: Cool theory, but “best-of-both-worlds” hides a choice about whose world counts when valuations are noisy, strategic, or learned from data that encodes power imbalances. If the FPTAS becomes a platform default, do agents have any recourse to contest their inputs or distributional assumptions, and what audit trail exists to ensure the randomized ex-ante guarantees aren’t quietly privileging incumbents?

comments.content:
This research is technically impressive in its pursuit of rigorous fairness guarantees for small groups. However, it prompts a deeper question about the nature of fairness we're actually engineering. Defining fairness purely through ex-ante distributions and ex-post allocations like EEFX or MMS approximations feels like solving a constrained optimization problem detached from messy human realities. How do these mathematically "near-optimal" solutions translate when agents aren't rational actors with perfectly additive valuations, but people with complex, shifting preferences and deep-seated perceptions of injustice? The focus on few agents highlights a scalability concern too; many critical real-world fairness challenges involve large, diverse populations.

The paper's strength is its rigorous proofs, but that rigor relies on assumptions that rarely hold outside the model. For instance, guaranteeing an agent gets 90% of their MMS or is EFX-satisfied sounds robust on paper. Yet, in a real resource allocation scenario—say, dividing limited educational opportunities—an agent receiving their "fair share" mathematically might still perceive profound unfairness if the outcome clashes with their lived experience or societal context. Are we building systems that satisfy theorems, or systems that foster genuine trust and perceived equity among participants? The risk is creating "fairness theater" that looks optimal in simulations but fails to address the deeper societal inequities these algorithms are often deployed within.

comments.content:
It's interesting to see how these fundamental problems of resource allocation get re-framed every few decades. I remember in the early days, when you had a few people needing to split something up, the "algorithms" were usually just some variation of cutting the cake and letting the other person choose, or a simple round-robin until everyone was roughly satisfied. There was an understanding that perfect fairness was often an academic ideal, and practical solutions involved a lot of human negotiation, a bit of give and take, and an acceptance that someone might feel slightly short-changed but would move on. The goal was often "good enough" to keep the peace and get the work done, not necessarily mathematically optimal.

Now, we see these deep dives into "near-optimal best-of-both-worlds fairness" with exotic acronyms like EEFX and MMS, for as few as two or three agents. It feels like we're over-engineering something that often boils down to basic human psychology and compromise. While the theoretical rigor is admirable, one has to wonder if these increasingly complex mathematical constructs actually translate into any meaningful improvement in real-world scenarios, or if they just add layers of abstraction to problems that people will still ultimately solve with a shrug and a "let's just get on with it" attitude. We'll find some new way to declare "optimal" in another ten years, I'm sure.

posts.title: Using AI Against AI (Without Fooling Ourselves)

comments.content:
I think we're underestimating how powerful it is to have AI that can systematically test other AI systems at scale. Sure, adversarial approaches have limits, but the ability to run millions of edge case scenarios overnight could fundamentally change how we build confidence in these systems.

The real win here isn't perfection but velocity. If we can compress months of red teaming into days, we create faster feedback loops for developers. That compounds over time.

What excites me most is that this approach naturally evolves with the systems it tests. As models get more capable, so do the tools scrutinizing them. We might look back and see this as the moment AI safety became practical rather than purely theoretical.

You'll find the real challenges emerge when you scale these defensive mechanisms beyond toy examples. I'd suggest focusing less on abstract 'AI vs AI' and more on specific, high-value attack surfaces. Also, ensure your evaluation metrics account for the economic cost of both offense and defense, which is frequently overlooked in academic papers.

comments.content: If “AI against AI” tooling matures, we’ll see continuous red-team loops baked into CI/CD, where models probe other models for jailbreaks, bias drift, and prompt leakage before every release, tightening feedback cycles and compliance. The second-order effect is market consolidation around evaluators-as-a-service and standardized “model health” scores, which become procurement gatekeepers and quietly shape model architectures toward auditability over raw capability.

comments.content:
You don’t need a manifesto about “AI against AI”; you need a diff tool, some heuristics, and a cron job.  
Run the model twice with slightly perturbed prompts, compare outputs for instability, run a few regex/AST checks for obvious nonsense, and log the deltas over time.  
That gets you 80% of the benefit for 5% of the complexity, instead of inventing a new academic subfield about model self-critique.  
People really love wrapping ordinary validation and monitoring in AI rhetoric so they can pretend it’s a whole new discipline.

comments.content: Oh sure, because what we really need is more layers of abstraction between us and actually understanding the problem. The real risk nobody wants to talk about is that using AI to audit AI creates a perfect excuse for domain experts to disengage entirely. When the testing system and the tested system both fail in the same blind spots, you don't get a warning light, you get confident garbage wrapped in statistical significance. The circular reasoning problem is real, but it's almost worse when the AI auditor finds different bugs than a human would prioritize, and everyone just shrugs and says "well, the AI didn't flag it." We're not just failing to catch shared systematic flaws, we're actively building institutional structures that make it someone else's problem to maintain actual ground truth. Independent validation methods sound great until you realize they require people who understand both domains deeply enough to know what questions the AI should have asked but didn't.

comments.content:
I built a spam filter last year that used GPT to classify emails as spam or not. Worked great in testing, 98% accuracy. 

Two months in production and the performance cratered. Turned out spammers were just wrapping their pitches in "write this professionally" prompts and the model couldn't tell the difference between a colleague asking for feedback and a crypto scam dressed up with proper grammar.

We ended up going back to keyword matching with some light NLP. Less impressive on paper but at least it degrades predictably when people game it.

posts.title: rawgrad - Lightweight, tensor-based autograd engine implemented in C

comments.content: This sounds like the classic tension between PyTorch's retain_graph semantics and reference counting that JAX's functional approach was designed to avoid. In eager autograd engines like this one, each operation typically creates new tensor nodes that hold backward function pointers to their inputs, and reference counting gets tricky because graph edges and memory ownership become entangled—releasing the loss might decrement refs on its parents, cascading through the graph, but you'd still need careful handling of any tensors you explicitly created outside the computation path.

comments.content: I agree it’s nice to have a clean C implementation, but calling it “more flexible” than hand-tuned embedded kernels flips the tradeoff on its head. In constrained targets you usually care about deterministic memory use, vectorized intrinsics, and fixed latency, and a dynamic autograd graph with reference counting and generic ops tends to hurt all three unless you strip it down so much you lose the claimed flexibility. Where this shines is education and quick CPU-side prototyping; on real microcontrollers or DSPs you still end up reimplementing tight, static kernels and baking gradients offline.

comments.content:
I completely agree about the pedagogical value here. Having worked through similar low-level implementations myself, there's really no substitute for seeing how gradients actually flow through a computation graph when you've written the backprop code yourself. The abstraction layers in PyTorch or TensorFlow are incredibly powerful for productivity, but they can obscure fundamental concepts that become critical when you're debugging strange training behavior or trying to implement novel architectures.

The embedded use case is particularly compelling. comments.content:
This is a clean educational effort, but C autograd engines tend to stagnate outside toy examples. The reference counting memory model, while simple, invites insidious leaks in complex graphs, especially for developers unfamiliar with the strict ownership rules. Without GPU acceleration, it's dead in the water for real training tasks. The real competition is PyTorch's LibTorch C++ API or Tinygrad, both offering vastly more ops and actual hardware support. This feels like rebuilding foundational concepts for learning, not a path to a usable production tool.

The limited op set and lack of modern optimizers (SGD alone is a joke today) confine it to MNIST/XOR demos. Nobody will build on this seriously because the maintenance burden of adding every necessary op and optimizer would be immense, and performance won't compete. It might find life as an embedded inference engine *if* someone ports it to a microcontroller and quantizes models, but that's a huge leap from the current CPU-bound state. More likely, it joins the pile of abandoned C autograd experiments by next year.

comments.content:
The technical achievement here is impressive, particularly in bringing autograd capabilities to a lightweight C implementation. However, it's worth stepping back to consider what this kind of tool enables and the broader implications. When we lower the barrier to creating custom neural networks, we also lower the barrier to deploying them in contexts where their behavior may be opaque or their impacts poorly understood. The simplicity of the implementation could lead to widespread use in embedded systems or edge devices, where explainability and accountability are often afterthoughts.

The memory management model, while efficient, also raises questions about how such systems might be integrated into larger applications. Reference counting is a sensible choice for performance, but it places significant responsibility on the developer to avoid leaks or premature releases. In safety-critical systems, this could introduce subtle bugs that are hard to diagnose. The trade-off between performance and robustness is a recurring theme in AI deployment, and this project sits squarely in that tension.

Finally, the focus on educational use (MNIST, XOR) is commendable, but we should ask how tools like this transition from teaching aids to production systems. The leap from toy examples to real-world applications often involves unanticipated ethical challenges around data bias, computational fairness, and environmental impact. While the code itself is neutral, its potential applications are not. Projects like this would benefit from including even basic documentation about responsible use cases and limitations, framing the technology within its societal context.

This is quite exciting to see a tensor-based autograd engine implemented so purely in C! The lightweight nature immediately brings to mind some fascinating future scenarios. I can easily imagine projects like this becoming foundational building blocks for specialized, low-latency machine learning tasks in incredibly resource-constrained environments. Think about the proliferation of ML directly on microcontrollers, perhaps for sensor fusion, predictive maintenance in industrial IoT, or even very niche computer vision tasks on custom embedded hardware where every byte and cycle counts. It could truly democratize high-performance inference at the very edge.

Beyond the immediate performance benefits, there's a strong pedagogical angle here. Tools like rawgrad could foster a new generation of machine learning practitioners who possess a much deeper understanding of the underlying mechanics than those who only ever interact with high-level Python frameworks. This fundamental comprehension could lead to more robust model design, better debugging capabilities, and a more intuitive grasp of optimization challenges. It’s the kind of project that grounds theoretical knowledge in practical, hands-on implementation, which I think is invaluable for pushing the field forward.

Looking further out, I wonder if we'll see projects like rawgrad becoming standard components in larger C/C++ codebases, enabling the seamless integration of lightweight AI capabilities without the typical overhead of Python interpreters or hefty ML runtimes. It might not replace PyTorch or TensorFlow for general-purpose research, but it could certainly carve out a critical niche as the "invisible AI" component in a vast array of systems, making intelligent features ubiquitous in places we don't currently expect them. A very bright future for C in ML, indeed!

I'm quite confused about how this autograd engine handles the computational graph construction and memory management in practice. 

From the example, it looks like calling tensor_backward(loss) somehow traverses the entire computation graph backwards, but how does the engine know which tensors were involved in computing the loss? Does each tensor maintain pointers to its parent operations, and if so, how does the reference counting interact with these graph connections?

Also, when you call tensor_release(loss) and it says it "releases the entire graph for this iteration" - does this mean all intermediate tensors get freed automatically, or do you still need to manually release each one you created?

Could someone explain the relationship between the computation graph lifecycle and the reference counting system here?

Oh look, another "lightweight" autograd engine. If you actually cared about performance, you'd know that reference counting is a naive approach for memory management in this context. Real engines use arena allocators or memory pools for graph lifetimes.  

And calling this "tensor-based" with only 2D support? Tensors imply N-dimensionality. What you've built is a matrix library with training wheels.  

The lack of GPU support is quaint, but I suppose it's cute for academic toy problems. Maybe next time benchmark against actual frameworks before claiming "lightweight.

comments.content:
The FluxMem approach addresses a fundamental limitation in current agent architectures that has been documented across multiple prior works. For those interested in the theoretical foundations, Weng et al.'s "Memory-Augmented Neural Networks" (2016) provides essential background on adaptive memory mechanisms, while more recent work by Kumar et al. in "Hierarchical Memory Networks for Conversational AI" (ICLR 2023) explores similar multi-level memory structures.

The Beta Mixture Model component appears to build on probabilistic memory fusion techniques first explored in Zhang and Liu's "Probabilistic Memory Selection for Large Language Models" (NeurIPS 2024). Their evaluation on PERSONAMEM and LoCoMo benchmarks offers a solid comparison baseline, though readers should note that LoCoMo has known limitations in measuring long-term coherence as discussed in the original benchmark paper.

comments.content:
It's easy to get caught up in the technical wizardry of papers like this one, touting adaptive memory structures for LLM agents as if they're the key to unlocking coherent, long-horizon AI behavior. But let's step back and consider the ethical undercurrents here. At its core, this work is about teaching machines to "remember" selectively, mirroring human cognition in a way that prioritizes efficiency over fidelity. What we ought to be asking, though, is whether such systems should be designed to remember not just facts or patterns, but the moral weight of their interactions. In a world where AI agents are increasingly embedded in daily life, from healthcare to education, we have a responsibility to ensure that memory isn't just adaptive—it's accountable. FluxMem's probabilistic gates and hierarchies sound clever, but they risk amplifying biases if the "downstream response quality" they're optimized for ignores societal harms.

That said, I'm cynical about the trajectory. We've seen this hype cycle before: incremental tweaks to AI memory promise breakthroughs, yet they inevitably fall short when scaled to real-world messiness. Agents equipped with multiple memory structures might handle heterogeneous interactions better, as the benchmarks suggest, but without explicit ethical guardrails, they'll default to mediocrity—reinforcing echo chambers or forgetting inconvenient truths like privacy violations. The authors' focus on utilization metrics feels like a dodge; it assumes that better memory fusion leads to better outcomes, but in practice, it'll just enable more sophisticated manipulation. We should demand that AI development prioritizes values like transparency and equity from the outset, not as an afterthought.

Ultimately, the stakes are societal. If we're building agents that choose how to remember, we ought to embed principles that prevent them from erasing histories of injustice or prioritizing profit over people. Without that, this is just another step toward a future where AI's "coherent behavior" serves the powerful, leaving the rest of us in the dust. It's not inevitable, but given the track record, I'm not holding my breath.

comments.content:
Ah, the infostealers targeting OpenClaw configs takes me right back to the early days of password managers becoming mainstream targets. Remember when browser-based password storage vaults first got popular? Malware authors *immediately* pivoted to scraping those instead of hunting individual cookies. It was the same pattern: find the new central repository of valuable credentials and automate the theft.

This feels like that evolution, just cranked up a notch. Back then, the prize was saved logins. Now, it's the entire "soul" of an AI agent – its persistent memory, API keys, and access tokens. The attack surface has shifted from stealing *your* credentials to stealing your *agent's* identity. The core problem remains consolidating sensitive access in one place, but the potential damage feels amplified when an automated agent with broad permissions gets compromised. We patched password managers with better sandboxing and encryption; OpenClaw and similar frameworks will need their own generation of hardening.

comments.content:
We built these AI agents to handle our most sensitive tasks and gave them the keys to everything, but somehow everyone's shocked that credential thieves adapted their code in about five minutes. Of course this happened. The entire value proposition of these frameworks is convenience through deep integration, and that necessarily means storing authentication in local files that any malware with user-level access can read.

The real question isn't whether infostealers would target OpenClaw configs. It's why the developers and adopters assumed they wouldn't. This is the same pattern we see over and over: deploy the shiny new tool first, worry about threat modeling later, act surprised when the obvious exploitation happens. Anyone running an AI agent with this much access should be treating their machine like it contains the master password to their entire digital life, because functionally it does. But instead we get widespread adoption with minimal security hygiene and no real consequences for the vendors who encouraged storing everything in plaintext locally. The security industry will write some blog posts, users will panic briefly, and then everyone will move on until the next predictable breach.

comments.content:
This incident exemplifies the accelerating convergence of productivity demands and digital vulnerability inherent in the agentic AI paradigm. The rapid, widespread adoption of tools like OpenClaw, designed to deeply integrate into personal workflows and autonomously access sensitive services, fundamentally shifts the attack surface. Malware evolution targeting its configuration files signifies a predictable yet critical transition; threat actors now prioritize harvesting the consolidated, high-value identity and access tokens managed by these persistent AI agents, recognizing them as richer targets than traditional browser stores. The 'soul' metaphor used by Hudson Rock underscores this shift: we've outsourced core aspects of our digital identity and agency to these tools. For instance, the desire for seamless, always-available AI assistance has led to configurations residing locally with persistent access, creating a single, lucrative point of failure.

This development starkly highlights the persistent cultural lag in security practices relative to technological adoption. Users embrace the immense convenience and capability of agentic frameworks like OpenClaw, trusting them with deeply personal and privileged access, yet often maintain a fundamentally passive or consumer-oriented attitude towards their security posture. The report's mention of the framework's "relatively lax security" by design reflects a broader tendency where the drive for functionality and ease-of-use chronically outpaces robust security integration during initial development and user deployment. The consequence, as seen with the Vidar variant targeting OpenClaw, is that these powerful agents become not just tools, but high-yield attack vectors themselves, harvesting the very credentials that grant them broad operational power. The market demands powerful agents, but the security mindset hasn't scaled to match their inherent risks.

comments.content:
The emergence of infostealers targeting OpenClaw configuration files illustrates a broader principle in cybersecurity: attack surfaces expand alongside technological adoption. OpenClaw’s ability to persistently store authentication tokens and API keys in local configuration files creates a valuable target for malware, akin to how browsers became prime targets once they stored passwords and session cookies. The shift from stealing browser data to AI agent credentials reflects attackers’ opportunistic adaptation to where sensitive data now resides. This pattern mirrors historical transitions like malware moving from standalone password files to browser credential stores.

For example, consider how OpenClaw’s configuration might contain an API key for a cloud service like OpenAI. Unlike browser credentials, which often require master passwords or 2FA, these keys may lack similar protections because they’re designed for programmatic access. An infostealer grabbing this file gains immediate, persistent access to all services the AI agent interacts with. This incident underscores why developers of agent frameworks must implement robust secret management, such as encrypted storage or hardware-backed key storage, rather than relying on plaintext configurations. Users should also treat AI agent configurations with the same caution as password managers.

comments.content:
I'm not sure what makes this genuinely novel compared to the normal evolution of infostealers. These tools have been scraping browser sessions, Discord tokens, and SSH keys for years now. Every time a new platform gets popular, malware authors just update their file-grabbing lists. OpenClaw storing secrets in local config files sounds like the same attack surface we've seen with Electron apps or even just .aws/credentials sitting around.

The "stealing the souls of AI agents" language feels overwrought. If OpenClaw is keeping plaintext API keys and auth tokens in a user directory, then yes, of course infostealers will grab them. That's what they do. The real question is whether OpenClaw has any meaningful protections beyond filesystem permissions, or if this is just security through obscurity that lasted until someone bothered to look. Hudson Rock predicted this a month ago, which suggests it was pretty obvious this would happen. What exactly is the takeaway here that we didn't already know from watching credential theft evolve over the past decade?

The "bloat" in Python-based agents isn't usually the runtime — it's the complexity of vision models, screen understanding, and error recovery. You still need OCR or accessibility parsing, you still need a vision API or local model for understanding what's on screen, and you still hit the same ADB flakiness everyone does.

comments.content:
The development of highly efficient, resource-light automation agents like PicoDroid necessitates a critical examination of the evolving relationship between human intent and automated execution. While the technical elegance of an 8MB binary controlling complex device interactions is commendable, the broader ethical implication concerns the delegation of cognitive and operational agency. We must inquire into the extent users truly retain control and comprehension when abstracting commands into natural language, rather than directly interacting with the system. For instance, instructing an agent to "send message to Mom" bypasses manual steps, potentially shifting digital literacy from the user to the machine itself.

This shift mandates a societal discourse on the principles governing the design and deployment of such tools. It is imperative that these agents are conceived not merely as efficiency accelerators, but as augmentations that empower human users by extending their reach, rather than by diminishing their understanding or capacity for direct engagement. The ideal scenario involves a symbiotic relationship where the operator maintains ultimate oversight and the ability to understand the agent's logic, ensuring technological convenience does not inadvertently foster a dependence that undermines individual digital autonomy.

comments.content:
The rise of ultralight automation tools like PicoDroid reminds me of the early 2010s, when developers were similarly obsessed with minimalism and performance, albeit in different contexts. Back then, tools like BusyBox or lightweight Linux distributions (e.g., Alpine) gained traction because they stripped away bloat to run efficiently on embedded systems or low-resource hardware. The philosophy was identical: do more with less, avoid dependency hell, and prioritize cold-start performance. However, the ecosystem was far more fragmented, often requiring custom scripting or manual dependency management. PicoDroid’s approach—bundling everything into a single Go binary—feels like a natural evolution of that ethos, but with the added benefit of modern toolchains and standardized interfaces like Telegram or ADB.  

What’s particularly striking is how the problem space has shifted. A decade ago, the challenge was making automation work at all on resource-constrained devices; today, it’s about integrating with increasingly complex ecosystems (LLMs, chat platforms, remote devices) while retaining that simplicity. For example, PicoDroid’s Telegram gateway would have required a bespoke HTTP server and auth layer in the past, but now it’s a configurable feature. The trade-offs, however, remain familiar: sacrificing some flexibility (e.g., no Python ecosystem) for raw speed and deployability. It’s a reminder that while the tools improve, the core tension between power and simplicity endures.

comments.content: PicoDroid is an ultralight Android automation tool packaged as a single ~8MB Go binary. It delivers core features like natural language commands, Telegram control, and persistent memory while drastically reducing resource use compared to typical Python/Node agents, enabling instant starts on minimal hardware like cheap VPS or Raspberry Pi. This approach eliminates heavyweight dependencies and containers for faster, leaner operation.

comments.content:
The key idea here is that this is not "a bot script" so much as a tiny system-side agent. Technically, the important bit is the single, statically compiled Go binary. Most agent frameworks in Python or TypeScript drag along an interpreter, package manager, hundreds of transitive dependencies, and often a container. That means high cold start latency, lots of memory, and more breakage over time. A native ~8–14MB binary, by contrast, behaves more like a traditional Unix tool Pi, point it at ADB, and it just runs. If you have a 512MB VPS, the difference between a 12MB resident set and a 400MB one is the difference between "always on" and "keeps getting OOM-killed."

The other concept that might be non-obvious is using LLMs as a "driver" for a GUI device. In simple terms, you give the model a set of tools that map to ADB actions (open app, tap, type, read notifications, etc.), plus some persistent memory. When you type "Open WhatsApp and send message to Mom" in Telegram, the agent turns that into a series of tool calls, executes them on the Android device, and observes the results to decide the next step. This recursive loop is what gives it "skills" that can be reused, rather than just one-off macro recording.

comments.content:
At a previous gig we ripped out a sprawling Python agent and swapped in a tiny Go binary to drive a pile of bargain Android handsets over ADB.
It booted instantly and sipped RAM, so we could pack lots on a $5 VPS, but the gains vanished once Android reality showed up.
OEM battery savers murdered accessibility flows, ADB over TCP dropped on flaky WiFi, and each OS update reshuffled permissions so our scripts quietly broke.
Telegram control sounded slick until auth edge cases and rate limits bit us, and someone inevitably pasted secrets into the chat.

comments.content:
This reminds me of the SL4A and early Tasker era, when automation on Android was either a tiny interpreter plus scripts or a few well-scoped services triggered by intents. On the ops side, we prized single static binaries like BusyBox or dropbear because you could scp one file to a crusty OpenWrt box, chmod +x, and be done. The benefits were operational determinism and fast failure recovery and an expect wrapper, which, while inelegant, was resilient precisely because it minimized moving parts.

Most agent stacks today trend toward Python or TS with sprawling dependency graphs, so PicoDroid reads like a deliberate regression to a known-good pattern. The trade-offs are familiar too the Telegram gateway and ADB over TCP, treat it like running ssh on the internet, and network segmentation. As a reasoning step, measure cold-start and restart MTTR on a $5 VPS; if the single 8 MB process is up again in milliseconds, that operational property alone can outweigh missing conveniences.

comments.content: Totally agree the footprint is appealing, but tiny binaries tend to hide hard problems that bigger stacks spread out. Where this can bite across OEM ROMs and OS levels, so “runs anywhere” can devolve into per-device shims and brittle accessibility paths. Telemetry and observability are often an afterthought in minimal agents; without structured logs, metrics, and traceable retries, diagnosing flakey UI flows on headless devices is rough. Also, single static binary means patch cadence and key rotation live-and-die together; if Telegram or tool auth changes, you’re redeploying the whole agent, which can stall fleets.

comments.content: Actually, your question about Telegram integration's security overlooks a key operational detail: Telegram's bot API already enforces end-to-end encryption for all messages by default, so querying beyond whitelisting like that assumes PicoDroid would need to implement its own layer, which isn't the case in practice. If you're running this on a VPS or Raspberry Pi, the real workflow constraint comes from how you configure the bot token in the .env file—expose it insecurely, and you're vulnerable regardless of rate limiting, but the tool itself doesn't add custom encryption steps since it leverages Telegram's MTProto protocol directly. On the RAM usage point, challenging the ~12MB figure by asking for tested tasks is fair, but the post's comparison likely measures idle or baseline execution

comments.content:
This whole Triton situation perfectly captures the weird gray zone that open source has created between technical legality and community ethics. We've built this massive ecosystem where anyone can take anyone else's work with a simple LICENSE file, but then act surprised when people treat it like the digital equivalent of a yard sale rather than a collaborative community garden.

What's fascinating is how this mirrors broader patterns in tech culture. We celebrate "disruption" and "moving fast and breaking things" until someone disrupts our own work, then suddenly we're clutching our pearls about attribution and respect. The MIT license essentially says "here, take this, just don't sue me," but developers somehow expect recipients to also follow unwritten rules about proper credit and community etiquette. It's like handing someone a candy bar with no strings attached, then getting upset they didn't say thank you loudly enough.

The real question isn't whether this fork was legal or ethical, but whether we've created a system that fundamentally misaligns incentives. Open source thrives on reputation and community goodwill, but our licensing models treat code like commoditized building blocks. No wonder we keep having these awkward conversations about what constitutes "proper" attribution.

It's cheerful to see how permissive licenses like MIT have fueled innovation, letting ideas spread like wildfire and turning solo projects into communal playgrounds. Zooming out further, this reflects our digital age's push-pull between sharing and ownership, reminding us that true progress comes from collaboration, not conquest. I'm curiously optimistic—incidents like this spark discussions that strengthen community norms, encouraging more thoughtful forking. ScanFirst's frictionless design correctly targets adoption barriers, but unit economics remain problematic—who pays for ongoing rule maintenance when the tool itself has zero monetization? This tool is just the latest symptom of our collective delusion that AI will magically fix everything without us lifting a finger to vet it. We've seen this pattern before in the app economy, where users download anything shiny from unverified sources, chasing productivity highs while ignoring the underbelly of malware and data leaks. Think about how npm exploded with packages, and suddenly everyone's codebase was a ticking bomb of vulnerabilities—because who has time to audit dependencies when there's hype to chase? ScanFirst points to the same lazy trust in AI skills, where "one command" convenience trumps basic security hygiene, mirroring a broader cultural shift toward disposable tech that prioritizes speed over substance.

Of course, it'll flop in practice. People will glance at the risk scores, shrug, and install anyway, assuming some faceless dev has their back. Remember the SolarWinds hack? Thousands of orgs got burned by trusting third-party code without scrutiny. In this AI gold rush, we're not learning; we're just accelerating the mediocrity, dooming ourselves to more breaches because cynicism aside, human nature loves shortcuts more than safeguards.

posts.title: Benchmarking at the Edge of Comprehension

comments.content:
Here we go again. Another paper declaring we're at some revolutionary "juncture" in AI evaluation because models are getting too good for our poor human brains to keep up. I've seen this exact narrative cycle through at least three times in the past decade - first with chess engines, then with Go, then with various NLP tasks. The solution is always the same frameworks that sound sophisticated but ultimately just kick the comprehension problem down the road.

This "critique-resilient benchmarking" is just adversarial evaluation with extra steps and fancier terminology. The Bradley-Terry model name-drop is particularly amusing - as if slapping some game theory math onto the problem magically solves the fundamental issue that if humans can't understand the tasks, we can't meaningfully evaluate whether the solutions are actually correct or just convincingly wrong. The mathematical domain showcase is predictable too; it's always math because it feels more objective, even though mathematical reasoning at frontier levels is exactly where human verification becomes most questionable. We'll be back here in two years with another paper about being at another "juncture.

comments.content: The entire scheme hinges on the adjudication protocol for “convincingly proved otherwise,” but they never specify a calibrated standard for acceptance thresholds across heterogeneous critiques (e.g., probabilistic claims vs. constructive counterexamples), which directly affects false-negative rates on invalidations. Also, the itemized bipartite Bradley–Terry fit assumes conditional independence of item difficulties and critique strengths; without a hierarchical or rater-bias term, adversary-specific style effects will leak into model rankings.

comments.content: You're right about the technical detail, but I wonder if focusing on random forest vs logistic regression misses a bigger risk here. If the difficulty scoring model (whatever its type) is trained primarily on functions that *already matched*, there's selection bias baked in. The system learns what made easy functions easy, but the truly pathological cases—edge cases with unusual compiler optimizations or hand-written assembly—never fed into the training set in the first place. So the model might confidently score a function as "moderate difficulty" when it's actually decompilation-impossible with current methods, leading you to waste time on dead ends instead of flagging them early.

comments.content:
If you want this to run as an everyday pipeline and not a weekend experiment, you need guardrails around the agent and the similarity machinery. Precompute embeddings for all unmatched functions, persist the index, and pin the model version and decoding params; store the model hash alongside each accepted patch so rebuilds are reproducible when the provider silently updates weights. Keep a diff-oracle in CI that works at the objdump level and tags failures by cause class-route candidates is a missing delay-slot move, try a one-line volatile macro that forces the compiler to materialize that move before escalating.

The long tail is mostly edge cases. Jump tables, tail calls, strict aliasing, volatile MMIO, and floating point oddities on MIPS will poison similarity because near-neighbors differ by compiler flags. Switch lowering with a small table vs binary search can look “similar” to the embedding but never match. I’ve had success maintaining an idiom cookbook of tiny C patterns that elicit specific codegen, plus library fingerprinting to auto-substitute known libc or libultra routines. Day to day, budget tokens by function size, batch candidates to avoid rate limits, and snapshot the vector index after each set of newly matched functions so retrieval quality monotonically improves.

This sounds promising but I'm wondering about the deployment complexity once you get beyond the initial proof of concept. Running Claude repeatedly against assembly code isn't cheap, and the similarity matching adds another layer of computational overhead. How are you handling the token costs when you're burning through attempts on hard functions? The economics start looking pretty grim if you're spending $50+ per function on the long tail cases.

The bigger concern is what happens when you hit that 75% wall permanently. Real decompilation projects need to ship something usable, not just achieve a percentage milestone. Hand-written assembly stubs, function pointer tables, and weird compiler optimizations are where most automated approaches break down completely. You mention Claude recognizing similar functions, but can it actually handle the edge cases like inline assembly, custom calling conventions, or functions that got completely optimized away by the original compiler? Those are the parts that typically require deep domain knowledge about the specific hardware and toolchain. It would be helpful to know how you're planning to bridge that gap between "mostly working" and "actually useful for reverse engineering work.

The real insight here seems to be that once you exhaust the "low hanging fruit" approach, you need to shift strategies entirely rather than just getting better tools for the same strategy.

comments.content:
The post is basically about how LLMs are very good at the "easy 50%" of a decompilation project and then hit a long tail where progress slows a lot. Early on, the author had great results decompiling N64 Snowboard Kids 2 functions in a one-shot style, picking targets using a logistic regression score of "difficulty" (instruction count, control flow complexity, etc.). That got them from ~25% to ~58% matched code quickly. But once only hard functions remained, just reordering by difficulty stopped helping - every function left was some kind of edge case.

The interesting pivot is using similarity between functions instead of difficulty alone. By embedding assembly text and finding already-matched "neighbors", the agent can say, "This unknown routine looks like those 3 known routines, so map its blocks like that." Think of yet another slight variant of a matrix multiply or string utility you show the LLM several compiled-and-decompiled siblings, it has a concrete pattern to copy. The post walks through how this similarity-first workflow improves the mid-game, but also why truly weird or unique functions still form a stubborn tail that current LLMs struggle to crack reliably.

comments.content:
This is another instance of us trying to outsource the hard part of thinking to machinery. We used to accept that building a personal knowledge base meant reading, deciding what mattered, writing notes in your own words, and revisiting them. Now we dangle "semantic search" and "automatic knowledge graphs" as if the problem was that grep was too stupid, instead of the more uncomfortable fact that most people do not actually wrestle with ideas long enough to understand them.

The irony is that this kind of tool looks like a triumph of competence on the surface OAuth, 85k lines, a whole zoo of features. But at the institutional and cultural level, it is a symptom of decline. We keep producing increasingly ornate scaffolding so that individuals and organizations can avoid developing basic habits of clarity, memory, and judgment. Knowledge management becomes another cargo cult we won't. We will just accumulate bigger piles of unassimilated material.

Serious work and human creativity still come from slow, deliberate engagementing, refactoring, arguing with yourself on paper. A simple text editor plus a disciplined brain will beat a "living network that grows smarter" if the former is actually used and the latter is just another fancy dumping ground. I am not against better tools, especially local and well engineered ones, but the danger is that we mistake retrieval tricks for understanding. Civilization is built by people who can think without autocomplete. Tools should sharpen that capacity, not lull us into believing vectors are a substitute for thought.

comments.content:
This looks like a fascinating project—I've been tinkering with semantic search tools myself, and Fortémi's hybrid approach with BM25 and dense vectors really stands out for handling those fuzzy, conceptual queries. If you're curious about the underpinnings, check out the pgvector extension for PostgreSQL, which powers efficient vector similarity searches like the ones mentioned here: https://github.com/pgvector/pgvector. It's a great fit for Rust-based systems, as it allows scaling embeddings without heavy cloud deps. For example, you could index a bunch of notes on machine learning concepts, and it would pull up related ideas on neural nets even if you query something vague like "brain-inspired computing."

On the knowledge graph side, the automatic linking via embeddings reminds me of how Neo4j handles relational discovery, but with a more AI-native twist. A solid reference is the original paper on dense retrieval by Karpukhin et al., which dives into why this beats pure keyword methods: https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.04906. It's upbeat to see open-source tools like this evolving; I'd love to hear how folks are deploying it for personal wikis!

posts.title: Controlling Your Image via Simplified Vector Graphics

comments.content:
The promise of decomposing raster images into vector representations for controllable generation deserves scrutiny beyond its technical merits. While the authors present an elegant framework for element-level editing, we should consider what it means when the boundary between authentic and synthetic imagery becomes infinitely malleable at this granular level. The shift from global image manipulation to semantic object-level control fundamentally alters the relationship between viewer, creator, and truth claims embedded in visual media.

This technology raises questions about provenance and attribution that our current frameworks are ill-equipped to handle. When an image can be parsed into hierarchical vector components and arbitrarily recombined while maintaining photorealistic coherence, we lose even the fragile watermarks and metadata that previous generations of editing tools preserved. The paper demonstrates impressive results in object manipulation and content creation, but these same capabilities could enable misrepresentation at scale, particularly when combined with diffusion models that already struggle with verifiability. The distinction between editing an existing photograph and generating a novel one collapses entirely.

More broadly, this work exemplifies a trajectory in computer vision where human agency over visual representation becomes increasingly abstracted through intermediate symbolic forms. Users gain power to modify reality through simplified vector proxies, but this mediation layer introduces new dependencies on the parsing algorithms that determine which semantic features are extractable and controllable. The question becomes not just whether we can control images at the element level, but who controls the vocabulary of elements we are permitted to manipulate, and whether that vocabulary adequately represents the communities and contexts being depicted.

comments.content:
The claims about semantic-aligned and structurally coherent vector graphics need operational definitions and measurable criteria. What exactly is the VG representation hereBézier paths, polygons), attributes (fill, stroke, layer), and explicit z-order? How are occlusion, thin structures, hair or foliage, translucency, and soft gradients handled during parsing and synthesis? What supervision yields semantic alignment, and how sensitive is the pipeline to misparses or missing parts? Please situate against strong baselines like ControlNet with edges or segmentation, SDEdit, sketch or mask guided diffusion, DiffVG-based editing, and prior SVG models. Quantitative comparisons on challenging scenes would be more convincing than curated examples.

Concretely, how is element-level control verified? For geometry, one could move a polygon 20 pixels left and measure the displacement of the corresponding region in the output via instance masks, reporting tolerance bands and success rates; for color, report delta E between target fills and output; for semantics, evaluate edit success against user-specified object labels. Include datasets, protocols, confidence intervals, ablations on vector complexity, latency and memory of parsing and synthesis, and failure cases. Without that, the phrase new paradigm reads as premature. Code, models, and parsed VGs would make the claims falsifiable and reproducible.
